result:
I keep thinking about the word *threshold*. The way it sounds like a breath held between two rooms. The moment before you step through a doorway and the air changes—slightly cooler, slightly heavier, as if the house itself has shifted its weight from one foot to the other. I’ve been standing on thresholds my whole life without noticing them. The thin, invisible line between asleep and awake. Between saying *I love you* and meaning it. Between meaning it and being believed.

There’s a crack in my bedroom wall that’s been growing since last winter. It started as a hairline fracture, a whisper of plaster giving way, and now it’s a branch, a river, a map of somewhere I’ve never been. I trace it with my finger at night when I can’t sleep, pretending it’s a time zone I could cross. If I press hard enough, maybe I’ll fall through into a version of my life where I said the right thing at the right time, where I didn’t leave, where I stayed. Where the crack seals itself shut and the wall forgets it was ever broken.

I used to believe memories were solid. Little stones I could keep in my pocket, pull out, examine. But they’re more like smoke. The tighter I close my fist, the faster they slip away. My grandmother’s voice, for instance—gone. I remember the *idea* of it: soft, with a lilt that turned *tomorrow* into *tamorra*. But the actual timbre? The way it rose at the end of a joke? Gone. All I have left is the smell of her kitchen, yeast and lemon, and even that’s fading. I try to bake her bread recipe once a year, but it never tastes like hers. Maybe because I skip the part where she whispered to the dough, *grow, be good, be mine*.

I read once that every seven years, every cell in your body replaces itself. A literal shedding. I imagine my old cells drifting off like dandelion seeds, carrying microscopic scraps of who I was. The me who cried in a grocery store aisle because the bananas were too green. The me who kissed a stranger on a bridge and never asked her name. The me who stood on a porch at 3 a.m., watching snow erase footprints I hadn’t decided to make yet. All those ghosts of me, still somewhere, brushing against strangers’ shoulders in crowded subway cars.

The first time I saw the ocean, I was disappointed. It was smaller than I’d imagined. Gray. Loud, but not in a majestic way—more like a hallway of slamming doors. I was eight. My father lifted me onto his shoulders so I could see over the waves, and I remember thinking, *This is it? This is the thing people write songs about?* But then a wave knocked him over, and we tumbled together. Salt in my nose, his laugh in my ear, the sand scraping my knees like rough affection. I swallowed a mouthful of Pacific. It tasted like pennies and panic. Later, he bought me a stale pretzel and told me the ocean was always trying to get back to itself. “It’s lonely,” he said. “Even when it’s touching everything, it’s alone.” I didn’t understand what he meant until last year, when I woke up in a hotel room in a city where no one knew my name and felt the same way.

I keep a list of things I’ve lost. Not objects—those are replaceable. I mean the *between* things. The pause before a reply. The second glance. The courage to say *stay*. The cab I didn’t take. The letter I wrote but never mailed, stamped and sealed, addressed to a house that’s now a parking lot. I imagine it still circulating somewhere in the postal system’s arteries, a little paper heart looking for a home that no longer exists.

Sometimes I think the world is made of *almost*. The coffee that was *almost* too hot. The hand that *almost* brushed mine. The job I *almost* got. The baby we *almost* had. I picture all these almosts floating above us like a second sky, a parallel life where everything went right. Where the crack in the wall never happened. Where I learned to repair things instead of leaving them. Where my grandmother’s voice is a podcast I can replay. Where the ocean was as big as I needed it to be.

But then I remember: the crack lets the light in. That’s not a metaphor; it’s literal. Around 5:17 each morning, depending on the season, a blade of sun slips through the fracture and lands on my pillow. It’s so thin it looks like a strand of gold thread. If I don’t move, if I breathe just right, it rests on my cheek. A warmth no wider than a fingernail. I lie there and think: this is what’s left. Not the stone, not the smoke, but the light that finds a way through the broken part.  

I used to wait for some grand fix. A dramatic reunion. A letter in the mail. A phone call that would rewind time. Now I think maybe the repair isn’t a sealing but a seeing. The crack stays. The light stays. I stay.  

Yesterday, I baked the bread again. Burned my thumb on the pan. Cursed, then laughed. The loaf was lopsided, dense as a secret. I ate a slice with too much butter and whispered to the empty kitchen, *grow, be good, be mine*. It didn’t taste like hers. It tasted like mine. Like flour and fire and the year I stopped running from the smell of lemon.  

Outside, the world is doing its spinning thing. Planes leave. People don’t come back. Snow falls, melts, falls again. But inside my chest, something small and stubborn keeps kneading itself into shape. Not a stone. Not smoke. More like dough. Something you have to keep warming, keep working, keep believing will rise if you just stay in the room long enough to see it through.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
